title: Security & Access Control
description: User management, roles, permissions, and security best practices

concepts.title: User & Role Management

sqlServer.description: SQL Server uses a two-tier security model: Logins at server level and Users at database level. Logins are server-wide, users are database-specific. Must create both login and user for database access. Windows authentication preferred when possible.

postgres.description: PostgreSQL unifies users and roles into a single concept - roles can have login capability. CREATE USER is shorthand for CREATE ROLE WITH LOGIN. Roles without LOGIN act as groups. Use pg_hba.conf for authentication methods.

concepts.keyDifference: No separate login/user distinction - roles handle both. Roles can be granted to other roles (inheritance). LOGIN attribute determines if role can connect.

concepts.title: Permissions & Privileges

sqlServer.description: SQL Server uses explicit permission grants on objects with hierarchical inheritance. Permissions can be granted at multiple levels. Schema permissions apply to all objects in schema. Use DENY to explicitly block permissions.

postgres.description: PostgreSQL uses a similar privilege system but with different syntax and some additional capabilities. Default privileges can be set for future objects. Row Level Security (RLS) available for fine-grained control. Use \dp in psql to view table privileges.

concepts.keyDifference: Uses PRIVILEGES instead of PERMISSIONS. Can grant on ALL TABLES/FUNCTIONS in schema. USAGE privilege required on schemas before accessing objects.
